questions.prompt: Complete: 'She suggested ___ the meeting until everyone had received the documents.'

options.text: postponing
to postpone
postpone
to postponing

questions.explanation: 'Suggest' is a gerund-only verb: suggest postponing. Using the infinitive 'to postpone' after 'suggest' is a classic learner error — 'suggest to do' is not standard English. 'Postpone' (bare base form) and 'to postponing' (a hybrid form) are both grammatically incorrect after 'suggest'.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'He offered ___ us move all the heavy furniture into the new flat.'

options.text: helping
help
to help
to helping

questions.explanation: 'Offer' is an infinitive-only verb: He offered to help. 'Helping' (gerund) cannot follow 'offer'. 'Help' is the bare infinitive without 'to' — wrong after 'offer'. 'To helping' wrongly combines both forms and is never correct.

questions.prompt: What does 'She remembered to call her mother on Sunday' mean?

options.text: She has a memory of calling her mother on Sunday.
She did not forget to make the call.
She regretted calling her mother on Sunday.
She tried to call her mother but failed.

questions.explanation: 'Remember to do' (infinitive) means not forgetting a future task — she had it in mind and did it. 'Remember doing' (gerund) would mean she has a memory of the past event. Option A describes the gerund meaning ('She remembered calling…'), not the infinitive. Option C confuses 'remember' with 'regret'. Option D confuses it with 'try to do'.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'He insisted on ___ the bill despite everyone's protests.'

options.text: to pay
pay
paid
paying

questions.explanation: After the preposition 'on', only the gerund is possible: insisted on paying. Any preposition (on, in, of, at, about, without) must be followed by the -ing form. 'To pay' incorrectly treats 'on' as an infinitive marker. 'Pay' is the bare base form. 'Paid' is the past tense — neither fits after a preposition.

options.text: I'm used to working long hours — it doesn't bother me anymore.
She is looking forward to receiving her exam results.
The coach made the players to train twice a day.
He gave up smoking after his doctor's warning.

questions.explanation: After 'make' (causative) in the active voice, the bare infinitive is required — no 'to': 'The coach made the players train twice a day'. Adding 'to' after 'make' is a very common B2 error. Note: in the passive, 'to' returns ('The players were made to train'). Options A and B correctly use the gerund after prepositional 'to' (used to / looking forward to). Option D correctly uses 'gave up + gerund'.

questions.prompt: Read: 'Rania had been cooking the same recipe for years. One evening she tried adding a pinch of saffron and the result was extraordinary.' What does 'tried adding' mean here?

options.text: She made an effort to add saffron but could not manage it.
She experimented with adding saffron as a possible improvement.
She regretted adding saffron to the recipe.
She stopped her usual routine in order to add saffron.

questions.explanation: 'Try doing' (gerund) means experimenting with something as a possible solution or improvement — Rania tested adding saffron to see what would happen, and it worked. 'Try to do' (infinitive) would mean making an effort, often implying difficulty or failure — but the text shows she did add it and succeeded. Option C confuses 'try' with 'regret'. Option D describes 'stop to do', not 'try doing'.
